misconceptionSummary: Students include 1 as a prime number and sometimes exclude 2.

lines.text: List the prime numbers up to 10

steps.narration: Two errors: one is not prime, and two was missed. These are the two most common mistakes with prime numbers. Apply the definition — exactly two factors — to both to see why.

lines.text: Factors of 1: just {1}  → one factor → NOT prime  ✗
Factors of 2: {1, 2}  → two factors → IS prime  ✓
2 is the only even prime — all other even numbers have 2 as a factor

steps.narration: One has exactly one factor — itself. One factor fails the prime test. Two has exactly two factors — one and two. Two factors passes. Two is therefore prime, and because every other even number is divisible by two, it is the only even prime.

lines.text: All composites — each has more than two factors

steps.narration: Primes up to ten: two, three, five, seven. Four, six, eight, nine, and ten are all composite — each can be expressed as a product of smaller factors, giving them more than two factors.

visuals.label: 1 excluded (one factor only).  2 included (exactly two factors: 1 and 2).

steps.narration: Two, three, five, seven. One is out — one factor. Two is in — exactly two factors. Apply the definition carefully at both ends of the list.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: listing 1, 3, 5, 7 as primes to 10. One has only one factor — not prime. Two has factors 1 and 2 — prime. Correct list: 2, 3, 5, 7.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Check both boundaries: 1 has only one factor so it is not prime. 2 has exactly two factors so it is prime. The correct primes up to ten are 2, 3, 5, and 7.
